One IP address does not mean one individual. In this dataset, we can assume one IP as 
one individual(or a group). But in general, IP address can be changed (like carrier), or same 
address can represent multiple individuals.

IP number 0 attempts connections with a relatively large number of ASNs compared to

A port scan is an attempt by a particular IP address to initiate random connections in 
order to discover vulnerabilities, which could subsequently be exploited for an attack.

IP number 1 was blocked before September. There is no evidence regarding the 
reason for its blocking.

IP number 5 disappeared after September 5. Although it may have been blocked, 
there are various possible explanations, such as the user discontinuing internet access. 
Based on the current data, no abnormal pattern is evident, so it is likely that causes other 
than blocking are responsible.

Alternatively, there may have been another anomaly that cannot be identified 
solely from the numerical data.

A Remote Autonomous System Number (Remote ASN) is a numerical identifier for 
an Internet Service Provider. For instance, this identifier distinguishes providers s
uch as Google or Amazon, which exchange traffic over networks (internet connections).
It is presumed that there is no correlation between the assigned ASN and the ISP.

A flow number indicates the volume of network traffic (internet connections) that occurs on a 
given date between a specific IP address and its Internet Service Provider 
(in this case, the Remote ASN).

To determine whether a specific IP address’s connection attempts are normal or anomalous, 
one must compare them to that IP’s average connection frequency. If an IP’s connections to 
a particular ASN deviate significantly—whether more or fewer—from other periods or services, 
an anomaly should be suspected.

IP number 4 has made an abnormally large number of connection attempts to 
service number 3671, suggesting a possible DDoS attack, in which excessive 
network connections overload the server.

A high volume of traffic (internet connections) is not inherently problematic, but if the 
frequency of connection attempts exceeds the normal average by a substantial margin, 
a DDoS attack—where excessive connections overload the network and disrupt normal 
internet activity—should be suspected.

When effectively all flow counts fall in a certain range, the outliers we identify (e.g., flows 
greater than 200,000) become more meaningful and likely to indicate unusual traffic. In other 
words, using the visualization to identify this normal range is useful for performing the task
 of identifying compromised workstations.

Each count is an instance in which a local workstation was connected to a remote IP 
address. A higher number of connections may indicate that a local workstation is compromised, 
as it is being used by a botnet in addition to the normal behavior of the user(s)

This dataset tracks internet traffic between 10 local workstations and remote access points 
for eight days. During the tracking, at some point, a few of the workstations were 
compromised and added to botnets. The purpose of the dataset is to use the provided 
data to detect when and if the workstations were compromised.

I don't believe so; the only data source that may pose confidentiality risks is the IP address of 
the local workstations, which the dataset providers map to an integer from 0-9.

The value of 'f' refers to the number of connections made between a local workstation 
('l_ipn') and a remote IP address/workstation ('r_asn'). It is very useful for carrying out 
the identified task of the dataset, as heightened or unusual traffic is a likely indicator 
that a local workstation is being used for a botnet.
